clinicalStem: Mrs S, a 46-year-old shop assistant, is referred to the gynaecology clinic with tiredness, lethargy, and iron-deficiency anaemia. Her menstrual cycles are 7/29 days with no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. Examination reveals a normal-sized, mobile uterus and no adnexal masses. She uses condoms for contraception. Which single investigation is the MOST important first step in her assessment?
options: Full blood count and serum ferritin
Pelvic ultrasound scan
Endometrial biopsy
Thyroid function tests
Coagulation screen
explanation: NICE guidance on heavy menstrual bleeding recommends a full blood count as the mandatory first-line investigation for all women presenting with HMB, to quantify the degree of anaemia and guide management. Ferritin confirms iron-deficiency status and directs replacement therapy. Pelvic ultrasound is indicated when structural pathology (fibroids, polyps) is suspected on examination, which is not suggested here. Endometrial biopsy is reserved for women with additional risk factors for endometrial pathology (e.g., age >45, treatment failure, persistent intermenstrual bleeding).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: FBC and ferritin are the mandatory first-line investigations per NICE CG44 for all women with HMB, quantifying anaemia severity and iron stores.
Incorrect: Pelvic ultrasound is the next step if structural pathology is suspected or if the uterus is enlarged/irregular, which is not the case here with a normal-sized mobile uterus.
Incorrect: Endometrial biopsy is indicated for women >45 years with persistent intermenstrual bleeding or failed medical treatment — not as a first-line investigation.
Incorrect: Thyroid function tests are useful when thyroid disease is clinically suspected (goitre, symptoms of hypo/hyperthyroidism) but are not the primary first step in HMB assessment.

Incorrect: Coagulation screen is appropriate when a bleeding disorder (e.g., von Willebrand disease) is suspected based on history (HMB since menarche, family history), but it is not the universal first-line investigation.
clinicalPearl: Per NICE CG44, FBC is the one investigation that must be performed in all women presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding, regardless of other findings.
keyClue: Iron-deficiency anaemia attributed to menstrual loss — quantify the haematological impact first.
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to pelvic ultrasound before confirming and quantifying the anaemia haematologically.
missedConcept: NICE-recommended step-wise investigation of HMB starting with FBC before structural imaging.

clinicalStem: Mrs S, a 46-year-old woman with heavy menstrual bleeding and dysfunctional uterine bleeding confirmed on investigations (normal ultrasound, FBC shows Hb 9.8 g/dL), wishes to avoid hormonal contraception. She is not considering pregnancy. After optimising iron supplementation, which is the MOST appropriate first-line non-hormonal medical treatment?
options: Tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menstruation
Mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily during menstruation
Norethisterone 5 mg three times daily on days 5–26
Combined oral contraceptive pill
GnRH analogue (goserelin) monthly injection
explanation: Tranexamic acid, an antifibrinolytic agent, is the preferred first-line non-hormonal treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding per NICE CG44. It acts by inhibiting plasminogen activation, thereby preventing fibrinolysis and reducing menstrual blood loss by up to 50%. As the patient has specifically declined hormonal therapy, options involving hormones (norethisterone, COCP, GnRH analogues) are not appropriate as first choice. Mefenamic acid (an NSAID/prostaglandin synthetase inhibitor) is also non-hormonal but is less effective at reducing blood loss than tranexamic acid and is more useful when dysmenorrhoea co-exists.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Tranexamic acid is the most effective non-hormonal treatment for HMB, reducing blood loss by up to 50% by inhibiting fibrinolysis. It is taken only during menstruation, minimising side effects.

Incorrect: Mefenamic acid is a second-line non-hormonal option; it reduces blood loss by about 20-25% and is particularly useful when dysmenorrhoea is also present, but it is less effective than tranexamic acid alone.
Incorrect: Norethisterone is a hormonal therapy (progestogen); the patient has specifically declined hormonal contraception, and it would not be the first-line non-hormonal choice.
Incorrect: The combined oral contraceptive pill is hormonal and contraindicated by the patient's preference. It is also relatively less effective for HMB compared to the LNG-IUS.

Incorrect: GnRH analogues are reserved for short-term pre-operative shrinkage of fibroids or bridging to definitive surgery; they are not first-line medical therapy for dysfunctional uterine bleeding.
clinicalPearl: Tranexamic acid is the most effective non-hormonal pharmacological option for HMB, reducing blood loss by 40-50%; it is taken only on bleeding days.
keyClue: Patient declines hormonal treatment — must select most effective non-hormonal option.
commonExamTrap: Choosing mefenamic acid over tranexamic acid when the question specifies 'most effective non-hormonal' treatment.
missedConcept: Hierarchy of non-hormonal treatments for HMB: tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic) is preferred over NSAIDs.

clinicalStem: A 46-year-old woman with heavy menstrual bleeding and a normal uterus on ultrasound has failed two years of medical therapy including the levonorgestrel intrauterine system. She declines hysterectomy and opts for endometrial ablation. Three months post-procedure she presents with worsening cyclical pelvic pain, no bleeding, and a tender, mildly enlarged uterus. Urine hCG is negative. Which complication has most likely occurred?
options: Post-ablation tubal sterilisation syndrome (haematometra)
Uterine perforation during the ablation procedure
Endometrial carcinoma developing post-ablation
Asherman syndrome with intrauterine adhesions
Pelvic inflammatory disease with pyometra
explanation: Post-ablation tubal sterilisation syndrome (PATSS), also termed haematometra, occurs when endometrial ablation destroys the endometrium but residual functional tissue in the cornual regions continues to bleed with no outlet, leading to cryptomenorrhoea. The hallmark is worsening cyclical pelvic pain with absent or reduced vaginal bleeding after a period of amenorrhoea post-ablation, with a tender enlarged uterus. This woman's history of prior tubal ligation (implied by the syndrome name) and the temporal sequence are characteristic. Uterine perforation would present acutely during or immediately after the procedure. Asherman syndrome causes amenorrhoea but not typically a tender enlarged uterus.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Post-ablation tubal sterilisation syndrome (haematometra) — cyclical pain with absent bleeding and a tender enlarged uterus after ablation is the classic presentation of trapped blood unable to drain due to cervical/cornual scarring.
Incorrect: Uterine perforation is an acute intra-operative or early post-operative complication presenting with haemoperitoneum or bowel injury, not a delayed syndrome months after the procedure.

Incorrect: While endometrial ablation makes subsequent surveillance difficult, endometrial carcinoma would not typically present as cyclical pain with a globally tender enlarged uterus at three months; abnormal bleeding or mass would be more characteristic.

Incorrect: Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions) causes amenorrhoea and infertility but does not typically produce a tender enlarged uterus with cyclical pain, as blood cannot accumulate behind a complete intrauterine obliteration.
Incorrect: Pyometra from PID would present with fever, purulent discharge, and systemic sepsis signs, not a three-month history of cyclical pain with no discharge in a post-ablation patient.
clinicalPearl: Cyclical pelvic pain with absent vaginal bleeding and a tender enlarged uterus following endometrial ablation is haematometra (post-ablation syndrome) until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Worsening cyclical pain with no bleeding three months after endometrial ablation.
commonExamTrap: Attributing post-ablation cyclical pain to PID or Asherman syndrome without recognising the haematometra pattern.
missedConcept: Haematometra is the commonest serious late complication of endometrial ablation, especially following prior tubal ligation.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman with insulin-dependent (type 1) diabetes attends the gynaecology clinic wishing to start a family. Her HbA1c is currently 68 mmol/mol (8.4%). She takes lisinopril for microalbuminuria. Which of the following is the MOST important single piece of preconception advice to give her FIRST?
options: Optimise glycaemic control to achieve HbA1c below 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) before attempting conception
Switch from insulin to metformin to reduce the risk of hypoglycaemia in the first trimester
Stop lisinopril immediately today and start a calcium channel blocker
Begin folic acid 400 micrograms daily and advise conception can proceed
Arrange retinal screening as the sole prerequisite before allowing conception
explanation: The single most important preconception priority in a woman with pre-existing diabetes is optimisation of glycaemic control. A target HbA1c below 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) before conception substantially reduces the risks of congenital malformations (particularly cardiac and neural tube defects), miscarriage, and stillbirth, all of which are directly related to periconceptional hyperglycaemia. Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor) is teratogenic and must also be stopped, but this is secondary to glycaemic optimisation as a first priority in counselling. Metformin is not a replacement for insulin in type 1 diabetes. Folic acid at 5 mg daily (high-dose, not 400 mcg) is indicated but does not precede glycaemic optimisation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Achieving HbA1c <48 mmol/mol before conception is the cornerstone of preconception care in diabetes as it directly reduces congenital anomaly rates, miscarriage, and perinatal mortality.
Incorrect: Metformin does not replace insulin in type 1 diabetes; she requires insulin therapy. Metformin may be used as an adjunct but is not the primary switch.

Incorrect: Stopping lisinopril is essential (ACE inhibitors are teratogenic) and this should happen immediately, but as preconception counselling priority, glycaemic control is the overarching first message; lisinopril cessation must also occur promptly.

Incorrect: High-dose folic acid (5 mg daily, not 400 mcg) is recommended for women with diabetes to reduce neural tube defect risk, but it alone does not make conception safe if HbA1c remains elevated.

Incorrect: Retinal screening is part of the preconception assessment panel, but pregnancy can worsen diabetic retinopathy; all organ assessments should occur within the broader goal of optimising metabolic control before conception.
clinicalPearl: The single most important preconception goal in a woman with pre-existing diabetes is HbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%); above 86 mmol/mol (10%) she should be strongly advised to avoid pregnancy.
keyClue: HbA1c 68 mmol/mol — far above the safe preconception target of 48 mmol/mol.
commonExamTrap: Prioritising stopping ACE inhibitor over glycaemic optimisation as the primary counselling message.
missedConcept: Periconceptional hyperglycaemia (not just intrapartum) drives congenital anomaly risk; HbA1c must be <48 mmol/mol before conception is attempted.

subtopic: Maternal and fetal risks of poorly controlled diabetes
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes becomes pregnant with an HbA1c of 72 mmol/mol at the time of conception. At her 20-week anomaly scan, fetal biometry is at the 85th centile for gestational age. She is concerned about risks to her baby. Which fetal complication is MOST directly attributable to poorly controlled pre-gestational diabetes in the first trimester?
options: Congenital cardiac malformations
Macrosomia with shoulder dystocia
Polyhydramnios
Neonatal hypoglycaemia
Respiratory distress syndrome
explanation: Congenital malformations, particularly major cardiac defects (e.g., ventricular septal defect, transposition of great vessels) and neural tube defects, are the complications most directly linked to poor glycaemic control in the periconceptional period and first trimester, when organogenesis occurs. The risk of major congenital anomaly is 2-5 times higher in diabetic pregnancies. Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and polyhydramnios arise from fetal hyperinsulinaemia secondary to sustained maternal hyperglycaemia predominantly in the second and third trimesters. Respiratory distress syndrome results from insulin-induced delay in surfactant maturation, an issue more relevant near term.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Congenital malformations (especially cardiac) are the hallmark consequence of poor periconceptional and first-trimester glycaemic control because organogenesis occurs at 3-8 weeks.

Incorrect: Macrosomia and shoulder dystocia are caused by fetal hyperinsulinaemia from second/third trimester maternal hyperglycaemia, not the first-trimester period that determines structural anomalies.
Incorrect: Polyhydramnios results from fetal polyuria driven by fetal hyperglycaemia and hyperinsulinaemia in later pregnancy, not from first-trimester poor control.
Incorrect: Neonatal hypoglycaemia is caused by persistent fetal hyperinsulinaemia responding to the sudden drop in glucose supply after delivery — a third-trimester and intrapartum consequence.

Incorrect: Respiratory distress syndrome due to surfactant deficiency is a complication of preterm delivery or insulin-mediated delay in lung maturity, relevant in the third trimester/perinatal period.
clinicalPearl: First-trimester hyperglycaemia causes structural (especially cardiac) congenital malformations; second/third trimester hyperglycaemia causes macrosomia, polyhydramnios, and neonatal hypoglycaemia.
keyClue: HbA1c elevated at conception — links periconceptional glycaemia to organogenesis defects.
commonExamTrap: Confusing macrosomia (2nd/3rd trimester) with structural anomalies (1st trimester) as the primary first-trimester complication of poorly controlled diabetes.
missedConcept: Timing matters: organogenesis anomalies reflect periconceptional/1st-trimester control; growth/metabolic complications reflect later pregnancy control.

clinicalStem: Mrs Huda, a 25-year-old woman, P4, is seen in the antenatal clinic at 20 weeks gestation. She has a history of poverty, poor dietary intake, and short inter-pregnancy intervals. Investigations show: Hb 6.9 g/dL, MCV 64 fL, MCH 18 pg, MCHC 27 g/dL. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis and which single investigation would best confirm it?
options: Iron-deficiency anaemia; confirmed by low serum ferritin (<15 micrograms/L)
Megaloblastic anaemia; confirmed by elevated serum homocysteine
Anaemia of chronic disease; confirmed by elevated serum ferritin with low transferrin
Thalassaemia trait; confirmed by haemoglobin electrophoresis
Haemolytic anaemia; confirmed by peripheral blood film showing spherocytes
explanation: The combination of low haemoglobin (6.9 g/dL) with markedly reduced microcytic hypochromic indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC all reduced) in a multigravid woman with poor dietary intake and short inter-pregnancy intervals is classic iron-deficiency anaemia (IDA) — the commonest anaemia in pregnancy worldwide. Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific single test to confirm iron deficiency; a level below 15 micrograms/L confirms depleted iron stores. Megaloblastic anaemia causes macrocytosis (elevated MCV), not microcytosis. Anaemia of chronic disease typically shows normal or elevated ferritin. Thalassaemia trait can mimic IDA but would be unlikely to cause Hb as low as 6.9 g/dL without significant haemolysis markers.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Microcytic hypochromic anaemia with poor diet and multiparity in pregnancy is IDA; serum ferritin <15 micrograms/L is the gold-standard confirmation of iron depletion.
Incorrect: Megaloblastic anaemia (folate or B12 deficiency) causes macrocytic anaemia with elevated MCV — the opposite of what is seen here; homocysteine is a research marker, not first-line.

Incorrect: Anaemia of chronic disease shows normal or raised ferritin with low TIBC — ferritin is elevated as an acute-phase reactant, which contradicts the clinical picture of pure dietary deficiency.

Incorrect: Thalassaemia trait causes mild microcytic hypochromic anaemia (typically Hb >9-10 g/dL) with a normal or elevated RBC count; haemoglobin electrophoresis is confirmatory but this is not the first-line investigation to order.
Incorrect: Haemolytic anaemia causes normocytic or macrocytic anaemia with spherocytes, elevated reticulocyte count, and elevated bilirubin — not microcytosis.
clinicalPearl: In pregnancy, microcytic hypochromic anaemia (low MCV, MCH, MCHC) with dietary risk factors = iron-deficiency anaemia; serum ferritin is the most sensitive confirmatory test.
keyClue: All three RBC indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC) are markedly reduced — this triad indicates microcytic hypochromic pattern, consistent with IDA.
commonExamTrap: Ordering haemoglobin electrophoresis first without recognising the dietary/social history and the severity of anaemia pointing to IDA rather than haemoglobinopathy.
missedConcept: Microcytic hypochromic anaemia in a malnourished multigravid woman is IDA until proven otherwise; serum ferritin is the confirmatory test of choice.

subtopic: Management of severe anaemia in pregnancy
clinicalStem: Mrs Huda, 25 years old, G4P3, presents at 20 weeks gestation with Hb 6.9 g/dL and confirmed iron-deficiency anaemia (low serum ferritin). She is haemodynamically stable with no active bleeding. She is asymptomatic at rest but has mild dyspnoea on exertion. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Oral ferrous sulphate 200 mg three times daily with dietary advice
Immediate blood transfusion with two units of packed red cells
Intramuscular iron (iron dextran) injection as a single dose
Intravenous iron sucrose infusion
Observation and recheck FBC in 4 weeks without treatment
explanation: For a haemodynamically stable pregnant woman with confirmed iron-deficiency anaemia at 20 weeks gestation, oral iron replacement is the first-line treatment. Ferrous sulphate 200 mg three times daily (providing ~65 mg elemental iron per dose) is inexpensive, effective, and safe in pregnancy. The Hb of 6.9 g/dL is severe but the patient is stable and not actively bleeding, so immediate transfusion is not indicated. Parenteral iron (IV or IM) is reserved for those who cannot tolerate or absorb oral iron, or who are in late pregnancy with insufficient time for oral therapy to work. Blood transfusion is reserved for Hb below 7 g/dL with cardiorespiratory compromise, symptomatic anaemia near term, or for acute haemorrhage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Oral ferrous sulphate is the first-line treatment for iron-deficiency anaemia in a stable pregnant woman; it is safe, effective, and NICE-recommended as the starting point.

Incorrect: Blood transfusion is reserved for Hb <7 g/dL with haemodynamic compromise, symptomatic cardiac failure, or imminent delivery — this woman is stable at 20 weeks with mild exertional symptoms only.
Incorrect: IM iron (e.g., iron dextran) carries a risk of injection site reactions and anaphylaxis; it is rarely used now and is not the first-line treatment for stable IDA in pregnancy.

Incorrect: IV iron sucrose is indicated when oral iron is not tolerated or ineffective, or in late pregnancy with severe anaemia and insufficient time for oral supplementation — not as first-line at 20 weeks in a stable patient.

Incorrect: Observation alone with Hb 6.9 g/dL is inappropriate; severe anaemia in pregnancy increases risk of maternal cardiac failure, preterm labour, and perinatal mortality and requires active treatment.
clinicalPearl: Oral iron is first-line for stable iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy; blood transfusion is reserved for haemodynamic compromise, severe symptomatic anaemia, or obstetric haemorrhage.
keyClue: Haemodynamically stable, no active bleeding, at 20 weeks — oral iron replacement is safe and appropriate.
commonExamTrap: Ordering blood transfusion for Hb 6.9 g/dL in a stable patient before attempting oral iron therapy.
missedConcept: Blood transfusion carries risks (alloimmunisation, infection, transfusion reactions) and is not used simply because Hb is low if the patient is stable; oral iron is the first-line approach.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman, G3P2, delivers vaginally at 38 weeks and suffers a postpartum haemorrhage of 2.5 litres. She becomes hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg), tachycardic (HR 130 bpm), and confused. Two units of packed red cells are being transfused. Clotting results return: PT 24 s, APTT 58 s, fibrinogen 0.9 g/L. Which blood product should be administered NEXT?
options: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 4 units
Cryoprecipitate to correct the critically low fibrinogen
Platelet concentrate (one adult therapeutic dose)
Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)
Vitamin K intravenously
explanation: The fibrinogen level of 0.9 g/L is critically low (normal in pregnancy >2.0 g/L; trigger for cryoprecipitate in major haemorrhage is <1.5 g/L). Cryoprecipitate is the most concentrated source of fibrinogen (and factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, factor XIII) and is the priority product when fibrinogen is critically depleted in disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to massive obstetric haemorrhage. FFP contains fibrinogen but at much lower concentrations; it is used to correct a prolonged PT/APTT but is not the best product for isolated severe hypofibrinogenaemia. Platelets are indicated when the count falls below 75 x10^9/L. PCC and vitamin K are used for warfarin reversal, not obstetric DIC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Incorrect: FFP provides all clotting factors including fibrinogen but at low concentration (~2 g/L); it is used to treat the prolonged PT/APTT but cannot rapidly correct fibrinogen as efficiently as cryoprecipitate when levels are critically low (<1 g/L).

Correct: Cryoprecipitate is the concentrated source of fibrinogen (each unit contains ~150-300 mg of fibrinogen); with fibrinogen 0.9 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage, cryoprecipitate is the priority blood product to correct life-threatening hypofibrinogenaemia.
Incorrect: Platelets are indicated when platelet count falls below 75 x10^9/L (or <50 x10^9/L if surgery required). No platelet count is given here and this is not the immediate priority.
Incorrect: Prothrombin complex concentrate is used for urgent reversal of warfarin anticoagulation or factor deficiency, not for obstetric DIC with hypofibrinogenaemia.
Incorrect: Vitamin K is used to treat warfarin-related coagulopathy or dietary deficiency — it has no role in the acute management of DIC or massive obstetric haemorrhage.
clinicalPearl: In massive obstetric haemorrhage with DIC, fibrinogen <1.5 g/L is the trigger for cryoprecipitate — it is the most concentrated fibrinogen source available in standard blood bank inventories.
keyClue: Fibrinogen 0.9 g/L — critically low, below the 1.5 g/L threshold that mandates cryoprecipitate in obstetric haemorrhage.
commonExamTrap: Giving FFP instead of cryoprecipitate when fibrinogen is specifically and critically low; FFP cannot correct severe hypofibrinogenaemia as efficiently.
missedConcept: Fibrinogen is the first clotting factor to fall in obstetric DIC and is the specific target of cryoprecipitate transfusion.
redFlagReason: Massive postpartum haemorrhage with DIC, haemodynamic shock (BP 80/50, HR 130), and critically low fibrinogen is life-threatening and requires immediate blood product resuscitation in an ABCDE-prioritised approach.

subtopic: Preconception counselling in T2DM — medication switch
clinicalStem: Mrs Zainab, a 33-year-old woman, P2A2, with type 2 diabetes is planning a pregnancy. She currently takes glibenclamide 5 mg once daily and metformin 500 mg three times daily. Her GP refers her for preconception counselling. With respect to her diabetes medications, which is the MOST appropriate management plan before she conceives?
options: Stop glibenclamide and switch to insulin; metformin may be continued or stopped based on shared decision-making
Continue both glibenclamide and metformin throughout pregnancy as they are safe in the first trimester
Stop metformin immediately and continue glibenclamide alone
Switch to sitagliptin (a DPP-4 inhibitor) which has a better safety profile in pregnancy
Stop all oral agents and begin a GLP-1 receptor agonist (liraglutide) for better glycaemic control
explanation: Glibenclamide (a sulphonylurea) crosses the placenta and is associated with neonatal hypoglycaemia; it should be stopped preconceptionally and replaced with insulin. Metformin is the only oral antidiabetic that has evidence supporting limited use in pregnancy (and is used in some centres throughout), so it may be continued or stopped based on shared decision-making — though NICE recommends offering insulin as the primary agent. DPP-4 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists have insufficient safety data in pregnancy and should not be used. The key principle is that insulin is the gold-standard for glycaemic control in pregnant women with pre-existing diabetes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Glibenclamide crosses the placenta causing neonatal hypoglycaemia and must be stopped; insulin is the preferred agent. Metformin has limited supporting evidence in pregnancy and can be continued in shared decision-making if the patient prefers.

Incorrect: Glibenclamide crosses the placenta and causes neonatal hypoglycaemia — it must NOT be continued in pregnancy. Continuing it alongside metformin would expose the fetus to significant hypoglycaemia risk.

Incorrect: Stopping metformin while continuing glibenclamide is the opposite of correct management. Glibenclamide is the more dangerous drug in this context due to placental transfer and neonatal hypoglycaemia.
Incorrect: DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin) have insufficient data in human pregnancy and are not recommended; animal studies show embryotoxicity. They are not a safe alternative.
Incorrect: GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to potential fetal harm based on animal data. They are not used in pregnancy at any stage.
clinicalPearl: Preconception: stop all sulphonylureas (glibenclamide crosses placenta → neonatal hypoglycaemia), stop GLP-1 agonists and DPP-4 inhibitors. Switch to insulin; metformin may be cautiously continued.
keyClue: Patient is on glibenclamide — a sulphonylurea that crosses the placenta and causes neonatal hypoglycaemia.
commonExamTrap: Thinking metformin is the dangerous drug in pregnancy when glibenclamide is the priority to stop due to placental crossing.
missedConcept: Glibenclamide is unique among sulphonylureas in crossing the placenta readily; it must be switched to insulin before pregnancy.

clinicalStem: Mrs Zainab, now 10 weeks pregnant with type 2 diabetes on insulin (started preconceptionally, current dose 75 IU/day, weight 75 kg), calls the antenatal diabetic clinic because she is having frequent hypoglycaemic episodes despite not changing her diet. Which is the MOST likely explanation for her recurrent hypoglycaemia at this gestational age?
options: Reduced counter-regulatory hormone production and nausea reducing carbohydrate intake in the first trimester
Increased insulin resistance due to rising human placental lactogen levels
Placental degradation of insulin leading to rapid drug clearance
Fetal uptake of maternal glucose is maximal in the first trimester
Gestational hypertension reducing renal glucose threshold
explanation: In the first trimester, insulin requirements frequently fall because the anti-insulin hormones (human placental lactogen, cortisol, oestrogen) have not yet risen to levels sufficient to induce significant insulin resistance. Simultaneously, nausea and vomiting of pregnancy reduce carbohydrate intake. This combination causes increased susceptibility to hypoglycaemia and often necessitates dose reduction. Insulin resistance caused by hPL and cortisol rises predominantly in the second and third trimesters, when insulin requirements increase by 50-100% above pre-pregnancy levels. Insulin is not degraded by the placenta to any clinically meaningful extent.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: First-trimester hypoglycaemia is caused by the combination of reduced anti-insulin hormones, reduced food intake from nausea/vomiting, and the absence of the second/third trimester insulin-resistant state.

Incorrect: Rising hPL (human placental lactogen) causes insulin resistance and increased insulin requirements — but this predominantly occurs in the second and third trimesters, not the first trimester.
Incorrect: The placenta does not degrade insulin to a clinically significant extent; insulin molecules are too large to cross the placenta and are not metabolised by trophoblast.
Incorrect: Fetal glucose consumption does occur but is not the dominant mechanism causing maternal hypoglycaemia; the effect is modest in the first trimester when the fetus is very small.

Incorrect: Gestational hypertension is not associated with reduced renal glucose threshold, and it does not cause hypoglycaemia. The renal threshold for glucose falls in pregnancy (causing glycosuria) but this would worsen, not cause, hypoglycaemia.
clinicalPearl: First trimester: insulin requirements FALL (hypoglycaemia risk) due to nausea and low anti-insulin hormones. Second/third trimester: insulin requirements RISE (hyperglycaemia risk) due to hPL, cortisol, and oestrogen.
keyClue: 10 weeks gestation (first trimester) with frequent hypoglycaemia — the first trimester is the period of falling, not rising, insulin requirements.
commonExamTrap: Assuming insulin requirements always increase in pregnancy without appreciating the first-trimester dip.
missedConcept: Insulin requirements in pregnancy are biphasic: they decrease in the first trimester then increase progressively from 16-28 weeks onward.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old primigravida attends the early pregnancy assessment unit at 11 weeks gestation for her booking scan. The ultrasound confirms a missed (silent) miscarriage with a fetal pole measuring 8 mm and no cardiac activity. She is haemodynamically stable. She is given information about all management options and asks which approach avoids surgery entirely. Which option correctly describes the non-surgical alternatives?
options: Expectant management (await natural passage) or medical management with misoprostol
Emergency uterine evacuation under general anaesthesia
Laparoscopic evacuation of retained products of conception
Mifepristone alone as the only pharmacological option
Intravenous oxytocin infusion to stimulate uterine contractions
explanation: For a missed miscarriage at 11 weeks gestation, the three recognised management options are: (1) expectant management — awaiting spontaneous passage of products of conception, (2) medical management — using vaginal or sublingual misoprostol (a prostaglandin E1 analogue) to induce uterine contractions and expulsion, and (3) surgical management — uterine evacuation via manual vacuum aspiration (MVA) or surgical uterine evacuation under anaesthesia. The patient specifically asks about non-surgical options, so expectant or misoprostol-based medical management are the correct answers. Laparoscopy is not used for uterine evacuation. Oxytocin alone has limited efficacy in the first trimester.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Expectant management and medical management with misoprostol are the two non-surgical alternatives. Both are valid evidence-based options; patient choice guides the decision after balanced counselling.

Incorrect: Emergency uterine evacuation is the surgical option — the question specifically asks for non-surgical alternatives. It is valid as an option overall but does not answer the patient's question.
Incorrect: Laparoscopy is not used for management of missed miscarriage; uterine evacuation is performed hysteroscopically or under direct vision (MVA/SVE), not laparoscopically.

Incorrect: Mifepristone alone is insufficient for uterine evacuation; it is used as a priming agent before misoprostol in medical management of later terminations, not as a standalone therapy for missed miscarriage in the first trimester.
Incorrect: Intravenous oxytocin has poor uterine stimulating efficacy in the first trimester (few oxytocin receptors present); it is not a standard treatment for missed miscarriage.
clinicalPearl: Three options for missed miscarriage: expectant (watchful waiting), medical (misoprostol), surgical (MVA/evacuation). Patient preference after informed counselling drives the choice.
keyClue: Patient asks specifically to avoid surgery — directs to expectant or medical (misoprostol) management.
commonExamTrap: Assuming all miscarriages require surgical evacuation, or that mifepristone alone is adequate medical management.
missedConcept: Misoprostol (prostaglandin E1) is the pharmacological agent of choice for medical management of missed miscarriage in the first trimester.

clinicalStem: The same 24-year-old primigravida returns 11 weeks later at 22 weeks gestation. An ultrasound confirms an intrauterine fetal death (IUFD). She is haemodynamically stable with no signs of infection. Coagulation screen is normal. What is the MOST appropriate initial management plan?
options: Cervical priming with mifepristone followed by vaginal misoprostol for induction of labour
Emergency caesarean section to deliver the fetus without delay
Await spontaneous labour onset, as expectant management is the standard of care at this gestation
Surgical uterine evacuation (D&E) under general anaesthesia
Intravenous oxytocin infusion as first-line induction agent at 22 weeks
explanation: For IUFD or missed miscarriage at 22 weeks gestation (second trimester), medical induction of labour using mifepristone (cervical priming, 200 mg orally, 24-48 hours before) followed by vaginal misoprostol is the recommended first-line management in haemodynamically stable women with normal coagulation. This approach achieves complete uterine evacuation safely and avoids the risks of surgical methods at this gestation. Caesarean section is not indicated for IUFD in the absence of maternal life-threatening indications. Expectant management is acceptable in selected cases but at 22 weeks carries a prolonged latency to delivery and an increasing risk of DIC (consumption coagulopathy) if retained >4 weeks. Surgical D&E at 22 weeks requires specialist equipment and expertise and is not universally available as first-line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Medical induction with mifepristone followed by vaginal misoprostol is the evidence-based first-line management for second-trimester IUFD in a stable woman with normal coagulation.
Incorrect: Caesarean section is not indicated for IUFD and carries significant maternal morbidity without benefit to the fetus; it would increase the risk in future pregnancies (uterine scar).

Incorrect: While expectant management may be offered in some settings, at 22 weeks the risk of maternal DIC increases with prolonged retention of a dead fetus (beyond 3-4 weeks) and active management is generally preferred.

Incorrect: Surgical evacuation (D&E) at 22 weeks requires highly specialised operative skills and equipment not universally available; it carries a higher risk of uterine perforation and cervical trauma at this gestation compared to medical management.

Incorrect: Oxytocin alone is relatively ineffective for induction of labour in the second trimester because the uterus has far fewer oxytocin receptors at this gestational age compared to term; prostaglandins (misoprostol) are superior.
clinicalPearl: Second-trimester IUFD: mifepristone + vaginal misoprostol is first-line. Beware DIC risk from prolonged retention; check coagulation if >4 weeks since fetal death.
keyClue: 22 weeks gestation with IUFD — this is second trimester, where medical induction (mifepristone + misoprostol) is preferred over surgical or expectant approaches.
commonExamTrap: Choosing expectant management as standard at 22 weeks without recognising the DIC risk from prolonged retention of a dead fetus at this gestation.
missedConcept: At >14 weeks gestation, the uterus responds better to prostaglandins than oxytocin; mifepristone + misoprostol is the standard medical induction regimen for second-trimester fetal loss.
redFlagReason: Prolonged retention of a dead fetus in the second trimester carries a risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC); coagulation must be monitored and active management initiated promptly.
